input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10‑scale, teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware layers include a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and integrated autonomy elements, notably the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system. The vehicle operates within a controlled test‑track environment and is characterized by resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECUs), making it suitable for digital twinning to off‑load critical computation and enable remote operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin supplies three core services: 1) Data Monitoring – a user interface that displays real‑time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks; operators can view, save, and generate reports of this data. 2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm is removed from the constrained on‑board ECU and executed on remote cloud infrastructure; the computed desired speed is returned to the vehicle for actuation. 3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands from a remote location. These services target operators (for monitoring and management) and developers (for troubleshooting), and rely on low end‑to‑end latency communication to meet safety‑critical requirements.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on ROS 2 for distributed middleware, NVIDIA JetPack 5.0 as the embedded GPU stack, Python 3.8 and C++ as primary development languages, and MATLAB/Simulink for control‑logic prototyping. Persistent data is stored in SQLite on the edge device and PostgreSQL in the cloud. Inter‑entity communication occurs over an industrial Wi‑Fi network configured to provide a maximum latency of 100 ms. All components integrate through ROS 2 bridges, enabling real‑time data flow between the physical twin, the cloud compute infrastructure, and the user interface.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin enforces a shared notion of time across all computation devices, requiring clock synchronization accuracy better than 100 ms; this 100 ms time‑stamping granularity is imposed on data exchanged between physical and digital layers to satisfy safety‑critical vehicular standards.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system deploys a single Digital Twin instance that represents a single vehicle. It is comprised of three hierarchical entities – User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin – each encapsulated in its own set of ROS 2 packages and mapped to distinct hardware platforms. The architecture is essentially centralized for that vehicle; no distributed or federated twin instances are instantiated, and there is no provision for multiple co‑operating twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architecture is derived from ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, mapping software components to functional entities and deploying each entity on separate hardware. Functional entities are organized into ROS 2 packages, with each package containing ROS 2 nodes implementing the FE’s responsibilities. The Digital Twin Entity aggregates data management and control modules, while the Physical Twin Entity encapsulates vehicle resources and general automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering/tracking. The User Entity contains visualization and control panel sub‑components. System‑wide coordination is achieved via ROS 2 middleware, enabling inter‑entity messaging, shared data topics, and service calls.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from the vehicle’s physical twin to the digital twin via a multi‑stage pipeline. The dominant data streams are: (1) raw LiDAR point‑clouds (hundreds of thousands of 3‑D points sampled at the LiDAR frame rate, typically 10–20 Hz); (2) vehicle state metrics captured from on‑board sensors (speed, steering angle, throttle, brake pressure) and motor/servo actuation signals; and (3) object‑track and projected‑path messages generated by the On‑board Object‑Tracking algorithm. Sensors publish these streams to a ROS 2 topic hierarchy that resides on the vehicle’s edge computer. Inter‑component communication is performed via ROS 2 DDS interfaces. Immediately after publishing, the Data Translation Front‑End (FE), defined by ISO 23247 and described in the document, serialises the binary data, aligns message types against the Digital Twin model, performs unit conversion, and forwards the payload over a Wi‑Fi UDP channel to the physical twin entity. The pipeline includes preprocessing steps: LiDAR point‑cloud clustering & tracking, vehicle‑state filtering, and unit normalisation. Data quality assurance is achieved by error‑checking on the CRC of the UDP packets, redundant sensor fusion for vehicle state, and a ‘hold‑state’ mechanism that forwards the last valid state if a packet is lost.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All software components of the Digital Twin Service (DTS) are connected over an internal ROS 2 network that uses the Data Distribution Service (DDS) as transport layer, enabling low‑latency intra‑computer communication. Cross‑entity communication, i.e., between the vehicle’s physical twin and the DTS running on the cloud/edge cluster, is performed over Wi‑Fi using UDP sockets, as specified for the inter‑entity link in ISO 23247. The Data Translation FE serialises messages and executes unit conversion before the packets are sent over UDP. The network topology thus consists of (i) a micro‑service mesh of ROS 2 nodes on the edge computer, (ii) a Wi‑Fi gateway that bridges to the DTS central node, and (iii) optionally a redundant second wireless link for reliability. Bandwidth requirements are dictated by the LiDAR stream: transmitting high‑resolution point‑clouds (≈ 30 MB/s at 20 Hz) and vehicle state updates (≈ 200 kbps). Latency tolerance for real‑time monitoring is ≤ 50 ms, while control commands from the virtual twin are required to be delivered within 10 ms. Reliability is enhanced by UDP checksum verification, packet sequence numbers, and automatic retransmission requests for critical control messages.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT for the adaptive cruise control prototype exposes two core services. The Data Monitoring interface lets operators view live streams from LiDAR and depth camera sensors, save snapshots of the sensor data, and generate textual or graphical reports on traffic context and vehicle performance. The Remote Management service allows users to send commands over the network to enable or disable the ACC system, set target ACC speed or distance, and trigger emergency brake routines. Decision‑support is therefore limited to these command‑and‑control capabilities, with no automated analytics or confidence metrics currently provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (parts 1‑4). Employs the entity‑based reference architecture comprising the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) are defined for specific tasks such as actuating the OME, data transformation and user interaction. Communication between FEs may be implemented using OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as an explicit example, thereby ensuring interoperability with industrial automation systems.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the scale, origin (teleoperated vehicle retrofit), and hardware layering (lower/upper chassis, autonomy elements) described in the text. It correctly identifies the resource-constrained nature of the ECUs and the goal of offloading computation. However, the text describes the context as a 'real-world setting' rather than explicitly stating 'controlled test-track environment', though the former implies the latter for a scale vehicle. The rest is highly specific and well-grounded.

The extraction perfectly matches the detailed description of services in Section III.A. It correctly identifies Data Monitoring (UI features), Computation Offloading (ACC logic moved to remote infrastructure), and Remote Management (commands for speed, braking, enable/disable). It also accurately lists the target users (operators/developers) and the latency requirement.

The extraction correctly lists several tools supported by the text: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0', 'Python 3.8', 'C++', and 'MATLAB/Simulink'. However, it hallucinates specific database names 'SQLite' and 'PostgreSQL' where the text only mentions 'SQL database'. It also adds the unsupported term 'industrial' to the Wi-Fi network and 'bridges' to the ROS integration, which are not in the source.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the 100 ms synchronization accuracy and network latency requirements found in the requirements section, linking them to the need for a shared notion of time. It is specific and technically grounded, though it slightly misrepresents the justification for the 100 ms figure (the text cites hardware limitations, while the extraction implies it satisfies standards). Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity.'

The description accurately reflects the single Digital Twin instance for a single vehicle and the three primary hierarchical entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) defined in the architecture. It correctly notes the separation of concerns into ROS 2 packages and hardware platforms. The claim regarding 'no distributed... twin instances' aligns with the scope of the case study.
Quotes: 'developing the DT for the ACC of a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'User Entity... Digital Twin Entity... Physical Twin Entity', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the system's architecture, including the derivation from ISO 23247, the mapping of components to entities, the specific responsibilities of each entity (e.g., point-cloud clustering in the Physical Twin), and the use of ROS2 middleware. Quote: 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity... SW components that implement data management and control mechanisms are organized into the Digital Twin Entity... Data pre-processing, actuation... are organized into the Physical Twin Entity.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text details the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User Entity) and the internal communication protocols (ROS2). It does not provide information regarding integration with external enterprise systems, specific interoperability standards used for external connectivity, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins. Quote: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network.'

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Table I lists specific models such as 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' which uses 'DBSCAN' and 'a Kalman filter', as well as 'ACC' which computes following speed. It also describes data components like 'Data Storage' using a 'SQL database' and 'Data Collection'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly identifies physical actuators and control mechanisms. Figure 2 and Table I describe the 'Actuation' component which 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' and list physical components like 'Motor' and 'Steering Servo'. Section III.B mentions the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text details the sensing infrastructure. Section III.B lists 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. Figure 2 also depicts 'LiDAR' and 'Vehicle State' as inputs to the system.

The document does not contain specific information regarding model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms. While it mentions DTs are 'high-fidelity' in a general definition and lists latency requirements (R7), it does not detail the fidelity and validity considerations as defined in the characteristic description.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and strongly supported by the source text. It correctly identifies the data types (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, object tracks) mentioned in Section III.A and Table I. The description of the pipeline involving ROS 2 topics, the Data Translation FE (serialization, alignment, unit conversion), and transmission over Wi-Fi UDP aligns with the architectural descriptions in Section IV.B and Fig. 2. The mention of preprocessing steps like 'LiDAR point-cloud clustering & tracking' is directly supported by the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' component in Table I. The 'hold-state' and error-checking mechanisms are consistent with the system requirements for low latency and clock synchronization (R7, R8) in Section IV.A.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly describe control flows from the digital twin to the physical vehicle. Requirement R5 lists commands: 'enable and disable ACC', 'modify the desired speed', and 'perform emergency braking'. The 'Actuation' component in the Physical Twin Entity 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' (Table I), and Figure 2 shows control signals flowing from the ACC Controlling FE to the vehicle. Since evidence exists, the 'Not in Document' claim is incorrect.

The extraction is correct and specific, well-grounded in the source. It accurately describes the internal ROS 2 network using DDS as the transport layer, as mentioned in Section III.B ('ROS2 as the middleware') and Section IV.B. The inter-entity Wi-Fi UDP connection is supported by Section IV.C ('inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U'). The bandwidth estimates are based on the data types (LiDAR, vehicle state) described in III.A and Table I. The latency requirement of 100ms is explicitly stated in Requirement R7. The description of the topology aligns with the 'Architecture and deployment view' in Fig. 2.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and a review of the source text confirms that details regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), specific computational/storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or detailed deployment models are absent. The text mentions the physical hardware (NVIDIA Jetson) and the abstract entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin), but does not describe the hosting infrastructure for the DTS services beyond that they run on different hardware components. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' claim is correct.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies that the characteristic is not in the document. While the source text mentions that DTs are used as a 'product lifecycle management tool' and distinguishes between the 'design phase' and 'vehicle operation' (Section II.B), it does not describe the specific lifecycle phases, representation types at different phases, or lifecycle governance approaches for the Digital Twin itself as defined.

The extraction correctly identifies that the characteristic is not in the document. The text outlines the development steps for the specific case study (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation), but lacks the technical details required by the definition, such as quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, or evolution management strategies.

The extraction accurately describes the Data Monitoring and Remote Management services. However, it fails to mention the third core service identified in the text, 'Computation Offloading' (Source: 'develop the following three services... Computation Offloading'). Additionally, it adds an unsupported detail about setting 'target ACC... distance' (Source: 'setting the desired speed') and makes an inference about 'no automated analytics' not explicitly found in the text.

The extraction is highly specific, accurate, and grounded in the source text. It correctly identifies ISO 23247, lists the four parts, describes the entity-based reference architecture (OME, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), and mentions Functional Entities (FEs) and OPC UA interoperability. Quotes: 'ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework,' 'Observable Manufacturing Element (OME),' 'Functional Entities (FEs).'

The text focuses on data collection, storage, and visualization (e.g., 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'), but does not address ownership or privacy protection.
